title: Factors and Multiples
blurb: Factors, multiples, primes, GCF, and LCM — built from rectangles and skip-counting.

lessons.title: Factors & Factor Pairs
lessons.concreteComparison: You have 12 cookies to set out in equal rows. You could make $1 \times 12$, $2 \times 6$, or $3 \times 4$ — but not 5 rows, because 12 won't split into 5 equal groups. The numbers that **divide evenly** into 12 are its **factors**.
lessons.objective: Find the factors of a number as the whole numbers that divide it evenly, and list them in factor pairs.
concept: A **factor** of a number divides it **evenly**, with no remainder. $3$ is a factor of $12$ because $12 \div 3 = 4$.
Factors come in **pairs** that multiply to the number: $12 = 1 \times 12 = 2 \times 6 = 3 \times 4$.
Find them all by checking $1, 2, 3, \ldots$ in order: the factors of $12$ are $1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12$.
lessons.keyIdea: A factor divides a number with no remainder. Factors come in pairs that multiply to the number.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting that 1 and the number itself are always factors.
commonMistakes.correction: Every number has $1$ and itself as factors: for 12 that's $1$ and $12$.

commonMistakes.text: Listing a multiple instead of a factor.
commonMistakes.correction: A factor divides into the number ($3$ into $12$); a multiple is built from it ($24$ is a multiple of 12).

steps.title: Arranging into rectangles
steps.teach: Try to arrange all $12$ squares into a complete rectangle. Change the width: when it fits perfectly (no gaps), the width and height are a **factor pair** of 12. Widths that leave a short row are not factors.

steps.title: Factors divide evenly
beats: A **factor** divides a number with **no remainder**.
$12 \div 3 = 4$ exactly, so $3$ is a factor of $12$.
$12 \div 5 = 2$ remainder $2$, so $5$ is **not** a factor.
On the grid, factors are the widths that fill a complete rectangle.

steps.title: Factors come in pairs
beats: Each rectangle gives a **pair** of factors that multiply to the number.
$12 = 1 \times 12 = 2 \times 6 = 3 \times 4$.
So the factors of $12$ are $1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12$.
$1$ and the number itself are always a pair.

steps.title: Finding factors
steps.intro: Check what divides evenly, and pair them up.

steps: A remainder means it doesn't divide evenly — not a factor.

steps.title: Factor or not?
steps.intro: Does it divide evenly?

steps.title: Listing all factors in order
beats: Go through the numbers $1, 2, 3, \dots$ in order, testing each one.
Every number that divides evenly is a factor — write it **and** its partner $n \div d$.
$18$: $1\times18,\ 2\times9,\ 3\times6$, so the factors are $1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18$.
You can stop once a number times itself passes $18$ (just past $4$): after that the pairs only repeat.

steps.title: The method, step by step
steps.teach: Tap **Test the next number** to try each divisor in turn. Every number that divides $18$ evenly is a factor — and it drags its partner in with it. Once you pass $\sqrt{18}\approx 4.2$ the pairs only repeat, so you can stop. The **factor rainbow** joins each pair that multiplies to $18$.

steps.title: All the factors
steps.intro: List each factor pair, then write them in order.

steps.title: Find a factor
steps.intro: Which number divides evenly?

steps.title: Square numbers have an odd count
steps.body: Most numbers have an even number of factors because they pair up. But a **square** like $16$ has a pair that repeats ($4\times4$), so it counts once — giving an odd total: $1, 2, 4, 8, 16$ (five factors).

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **factor** divides a number with no remainder.
Factors come in **pairs** that multiply to the number: $3 \times 4 = 12$.
List them by checking $1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and pairing as you go.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Time to put your factor skills to work with a mix of problems on factors and factor pairs.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Which number is a factor of $24$?
problems.explanation: $6$ divides $24$ evenly because $24 \div 6 = 4$ with no remainder.

problems.prompt: Which of these is NOT a factor of $40$?
problems.explanation: $40 \div 6$ leaves a remainder, so $6$ is not a factor of $40$.

problems.prompt: Which pair of numbers is a factor pair of $42$?
problems.explanation: $6 \times 7 = 42$, so $6$ and $7$ are a factor pair of $42$.

problems.prompt: How many factors does $48$ have? (Hint: list its factor pairs.)
problems.explanation: The factors of $48$ are $1,2,3,4,6,8,12,16,24,48$, which is $10$ factors.

problems.prompt: A number has the factor pair $8 \times 9$. What is the number, and which of these is also one of its factors?
problems.explanation: Since $8 \times 9 = 72$ and $72 \div 6 = 12$, the number is $72$ and $6$ is a factor.

steps.title: You've finished "Factors & Factor Pairs"
steps.body: Nice work, you've mastered finding factors and factor pairs. Next up is "Multiples," where you'll flip the idea around and skip-count to build numbers instead of breaking them down.

lessons.title: Multiples
lessons.concreteComparison: Counting off a marching band in 4s — $4, 8, 12, 16, \ldots$ — lists the **multiples** of 4. A multiple of a number is what you land on when you skip-count by it (or multiply it by $1, 2, 3, \ldots$).
lessons.objective: Find multiples of a number by skip-counting (multiplying by 1, 2, 3, …), and find common multiples shared by two numbers.
concept: A **multiple** of a number is that number times $1, 2, 3, \ldots$ — what you land on skip-counting. Multiples of $4$: $4, 8, 12, 16, \ldots$
Every number has **infinitely many** multiples, and is its own smallest (nonzero) multiple.
A **common multiple** of two numbers shows up in **both** lists. $12$ is a common multiple of $3$ and $4$.
lessons.keyIdea: A multiple is the number × 1, 2, 3, … (skip-counting). Common multiples appear in both numbers' lists.

workedExamples.statement: List the first four multiples of $6$.
Find a common multiple of $3$ and $4$.

commonMistakes.correction: A multiple is **bigger** (built from the number): $12$ is a multiple of $3$. A factor **divides** it: $3$ is a factor of $12$.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking a number has only a few multiples.
commonMistakes.correction: Multiples never run out — you can always skip-count one more.

tryIt.statement: Find a common multiple of $2$ and $5$.

steps.title: Skip-counting lights up multiples
steps.teach: Pick a number and skip-count by it. Every square you land on — $k, 2k, 3k, \ldots$ — is a **multiple** of $k$. Change the number and watch the pattern shift.

steps.title: Multiples are skip-counting
beats: A multiple of $4$ is $4 \times 1, 4 \times 2, 4 \times 3, \ldots$
That lands you on $4, 8, 12, 16, \ldots$
The list never ends — there are infinitely many.
The number itself ($4$) is its smallest nonzero multiple.

steps.title: Counting in equal groups
beats: A multiple is what you get by counting in **equal groups**.
$3$ eggs per row: $1$ row is $3$, $2$ rows is $6$, $3$ rows is $9$ — the multiples of $3$.
So a number's multiples are just its **times-table**: $3, 6, 9, 12, \ldots$
Because the list is built by multiplying, every multiple of $3$ has $3$ as a **factor** — the flip side of the coin.

steps.title: Factors vs multiples
beats: A **factor** divides **into** a number: $3$ is a factor of $12$.
A **multiple** is built **from** a number: $12$ is a multiple of $3$.
Factors are $\leq$ the number; multiples are $\geq$ the number.
$3$ and $12$ show both sides of the same relationship.

steps.title: Listing multiples
steps.intro: Multiply by 1, 2, 3, 4, …

examples.prompt: List the first four multiples of $6$.
List the first four multiples of $5$.

steps.title: Is it a multiple?
steps.intro: Does the number divide it evenly?

steps.title: Common multiples
beats: List the multiples of each number, then look for matches.
Multiples of $3$: $3, 6, 9, 12, \ldots$
Multiples of $4$: $4, 8, 12, \ldots$
$12$ shows up in both — a **common multiple** of $3$ and $4$.

steps.title: Common multiples
steps.intro: Find a number that appears in both lists.

examples.prompt: Find a common multiple of $3$ and $4$.
Find a common multiple of $2$ and $5$.

steps.title: Find a common multiple
steps.intro: Which number is in both lists?

problems.prompt: Which is a common multiple of $3$ and $4$?
problems.explanation: $12$ is a multiple of both $3$ and $4$.

problems.prompt: Which is a common multiple of $2$ and $5$?
problems.explanation: $10$ is a multiple of both $2$ and $5$.

steps.title: When skip-counts collide
steps.body: Skip-count by 3 and by 4 at the same time and you both land on $12$ — then $24$, then $36$. The first place two skip-counts meet is their **least common multiple**, the trick behind adding fractions with different denominators.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **multiple** is the number × $1, 2, 3, \ldots$ — skip-counting, forever.
A **common multiple** appears in both numbers' lists, like $12$ for $3$ and $4$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review multiples and common multiples with a mix of skip-counting and odd-one-out problems.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Which number is a multiple of $7$?
Which number is NOT a multiple of $7$?
problems.explanation: $34$ is not in the 7 times table, since $7 \times 4 = 28$ and $7 \times 5 = 35$.

problems.prompt: Counting by $9$, what is the $5$th multiple of $9$?
problems.explanation: Skip-counting gives $9, 18, 27, 36, 45$, so the 5th multiple is 45.

problems.prompt: Which number is a common multiple of both $4$ and $6$?
problems.explanation: $24 = 4 \times 6$ and $24 = 6 \times 4$, so it appears in both lists.

problems.prompt: Which number is a common multiple of both $4$ and $10$?
problems.explanation: $60$ is in both lists since $60 = 4 \times 15$ and $60 = 10 \times 6$.

steps.title: You've finished "Multiples"
steps.body: Great work, you've finished the Multiples lesson. You can now list multiples by skip-counting and spot the common multiples shared by two numbers.

lessons.title: Prime & Composite Numbers
lessons.concreteComparison: Arrange chairs into equal rows. With $12$ chairs you can make $2\times6$, $3\times4$, $4\times3$ — lots of neat rectangles. With $7$ chairs the only neat rectangle is a single row, $1\times7$. A number like $7$ that can **only** be a single row is **prime**; one like $12$ with extra arrangements is **composite**.
lessons.objective: Classify a whole number as prime (exactly two factors: 1 and itself) or composite (more than two factors), and explain why 1 is neither.
concept: A **prime** number has exactly **two** factors: $1$ and itself. $7$ divides only by $1$ and $7$.
A **composite** number has **more than two** factors — it can be built from smaller numbers. $12 = 2\times6$.
**$1$ is neither** prime nor composite: it has only **one** factor, itself.
lessons.keyIdea: Prime = exactly two factors (1 and itself). Composite = more than two. 1 is neither.

facts.title: The only even prime
facts.explanation: Every other even number also has 2 as a factor.

workedExamples.solution: The only factors of $11$ are $1$ and $11$ — exactly two — so it is prime.
$15 = 3\times5$, so its factors are $1, 3, 5, 15$ — more than two. Composite.

commonMistakes.correction: $1$ has only **one** factor (itself), so it is **neither** prime nor composite.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming every odd number is prime.
commonMistakes.correction: $9$ is odd but $9 = 3\times3$, so it is composite.

tryIt.solution: Its only factors are $1$ and $2$, so yes — and it's the only even prime.

steps.title: Count the factors
steps.teach: Tap any number to see its factors. **Exactly two** factors — $1$ and itself — means **prime** (highlighted). **More than two** means **composite**.

steps.title: What makes a number prime
beats: A **prime** has exactly two factors: $1$ and itself.
$7$ divides only by $1$ and $7$ — prime.
A **composite** has more than two factors.

steps.title: Primes are the building blocks
beats: A **composite** number can be broken into a product of smaller numbers.
A **prime** cannot be broken down — its only factors are $1$ and itself.
So primes are the **building blocks** every whole number is made from.
$12 = 2\times 2\times 3$ — all primes; $7$ is already a single block.

steps.title: Where does 1 go?
beats: $1$ has only **one** factor: itself.
Prime needs **two** factors, so $1$ isn't prime.
Composite needs **more than two**, so $1$ isn't composite.
$1$ is **neither** — it sits on its own.

steps.title: Prime or composite?
steps.intro: Count the factors.

steps.title: Prime or composite?
steps.intro: Does any smaller number divide it?

steps.title: Scan for a factor
beats: Check whether a smaller number divides it evenly.
If one does, it's **composite**.
$2$ is the only even prime — every other even number has $2$ as a factor.
Quick scan: even? divisible by $3$? by $5$?

steps.title: Spotting factors fast
steps.intro: Look for an easy divisor first.

steps: $2+7=9$ is divisible by $3$, so $27$ is too.
Not even; not divisible by $3$ or $5$.

steps.title: Find the odd one out
steps.intro: Use the quick test.

problems.explanation: $19$ has no factor but $1$ and $19$; the others split.

steps.title: The atoms of arithmetic
steps.body: Primes are the building blocks of every other number, and they never run out — the list $2, 3, 5, 7, 11, \ldots$ goes on forever. Over $2{,}000$ years ago Euclid proved there is no largest prime, and that very fact is what keeps your online passwords safe today.

steps.title: Is it prime?
steps.explanation: No smaller number divides $29$ evenly, so it's prime.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **$1$ is neither**; **$2$ is the only even prime.**

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review prime and composite with a mix of classify, odd-one-out, and that tricky number $1$.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.explanation: $29$ has no factor but $1$ and itself.
$1$ has only one factor, so it is neither.

problems.prompt: Which is the only even prime number?
problems.explanation: Every even number past $2$ has $2$ as an extra factor.

steps.title: You've finished "Prime & Composite Numbers"
steps.body: Great work — you can now tell primes from composites and you know $1$ is in a class of its own. Next up: Greatest Common Factor, where shared factors do the heavy lifting.

lessons.title: Greatest Common Factor
lessons.concreteComparison: You have $12$ granola bars and $18$ juice boxes and want identical snack packs with nothing left over. The most packs you can make is the **greatest common factor** of $12$ and $18$ — the largest number that divides **both** ($6$ packs, each with $2$ bars and $3$ juices).
lessons.objective: Find the greatest common factor (GCF) of two numbers by listing factors and choosing the largest one they share.
concept: A **common factor** divides **both** numbers; $1$ is always one.
The **greatest common factor (GCF)** is the **largest** common factor.
List each number's factors, find the shared ones, and take the biggest.
lessons.keyIdea: GCF = the largest factor two numbers share. List factors of each and pick the greatest in both lists.

workedExamples.solution: Factors of $12$: $1,2,3,4,6,12$. Factors of $18$: $1,2,3,6,9,18$. The largest shared one is $6$.
Shared factors are $1,2,4$; the greatest is $4$.

commonMistakes.text: Stopping at a common factor that isn't the greatest.
commonMistakes.correction: $2$ divides both $12$ and $18$, but $6$ is larger — take the **greatest**.
The GCF is a **factor** (no bigger than either number); the LCM is a **multiple** (no smaller than either).

tryIt.statement: What is the GCF of $6$ and $9$?
tryIt.solution: Shared factors are $1, 3$; the greatest is $3$.

tryIt.statement: What is the GCF of $10$ and $15$?
tryIt.solution: Shared factors are $1, 5$; the greatest is $5$.

steps.title: Where factor lists overlap
steps.teach: The chips are each number's factors. The **highlighted** ones are shared, and the **ringed** chip is the largest shared factor — the GCF. Change either number and watch the overlap shift.

steps.title: List the factors
beats: Write every factor of each number.
Now compare the two lists.

steps.title: Take the greatest shared one
beats: Shared factors of $12$ and $18$: $1, 2, 3, 6$.
These are the **common factors**.
The largest is $6$.

steps.title: Finding the GCF
steps.intro: List, compare, take the greatest.

steps.title: Find the GCF
steps.intro: Largest factor in both lists.

problems.explanation: Shared factors are $1, 2$; the greatest is $2$.
Shared factors include $1,2,5,10$; the greatest is $10$.
Both divide by $7$ and nothing larger.

steps.title: When the GCF is 1
beats: Sometimes the only shared factor is $1$.
Factors of $7$: $1, 7$. Factors of $9$: $1, 3, 9$.
The only one in both is $1$.
Then $\text{GCF} = 1$ — the numbers are **relatively prime**.

steps.title: GCF of 1
steps.intro: No shared factor but 1.

steps.title: Greatest, not just common
steps.intro: Watch for the largest.

problems.prompt: Which is the GCF of $24$ and $36$?
problems.explanation: $6$ is common, but $12$ is the greatest shared factor.

steps.title: The fraction-shrinking trick
steps.body: Dividing the top and bottom of a fraction by their GCF reduces it in one move: $\tfrac{12}{18}$ has $\text{GCF}(12,18)=6$, so $\tfrac{12}{18} = \tfrac{2}{3}$ straight away — no step-by-step halving needed.

steps.title: Greatest shared factor
steps.prompt: What is the GCF of $18$ and $24$?
steps.explanation: Shared factors are $1,2,3,6$; the greatest is $6$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: List factors of each, take the greatest shared one. If only $1$ is shared, the GCF is $1$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review the GCF with straight finds, a watch-for-the-greatest trap, and a real-world split.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.explanation: Both divide by $8$ and nothing larger.

problems.prompt: $5$ is a common factor of $20$ and $30$. Is it the GCF?
problems.explanation: $10$ also divides both and is larger than $5$.
$12$ is the largest factor of both.

problems.prompt: You have $15$ red and $25$ blue beads to split into identical bags with none left over. Most bags?

steps.title: You've finished "Greatest Common Factor"
steps.body: Nicely done — you can list factors, spot the shared ones, and grab the greatest. Next: the Least Common Multiple, the mirror-image idea built from multiples instead of factors.

lessons.title: Least Common Multiple
lessons.concreteComparison: Hot dogs come in packs of $6$ and buns in packs of $8$. To have no leftovers you need a number that is a multiple of **both** — and the smallest such number is the **least common multiple** of $6$ and $8$, which is $24$ ($4$ hot-dog packs and $3$ bun packs).
lessons.objective: Find the least common multiple (LCM) of two numbers by listing multiples and choosing the smallest one they share.
concept: A **common multiple** appears in **both** numbers' multiple lists.
The **least common multiple (LCM)** is the **smallest** of these.
List multiples of each, skip-counting, until one matches.
lessons.keyIdea: LCM = the smallest multiple two numbers share. List multiples of each until one appears in both.

workedExamples.solution: Multiples of $4$: $4,8,12$. Multiples of $6$: $6,12$. The first shared one is $12$.

commonMistakes.correction: $4\times6 = 24$, but the **least** common multiple is $12$. Multiplying gives *a* common multiple, not always the smallest.
The LCM is a **multiple** (at least as big as either number); the GCF is a **factor** (no bigger than either).

tryIt.statement: What is the LCM of $2$ and $5$?
What is the LCM of $6$ and $9$?

steps.title: Where skip-counts first meet
steps.teach: Each strip shows a number's multiples. The **highlighted** ones appear in both strips, and the **ringed** one is the first (smallest) — the LCM. Change either number and watch where the two skip-counts collide.

steps.title: List the multiples
beats: Skip-count each number a few times.
Multiples of $4$: $4, 8, 12, 16, \ldots$
Multiples of $6$: $6, 12, 18, \ldots$
Then look for the first match.

steps.title: Take the smallest shared one
beats: $12$ shows up in both lists.
It's the **first** place they meet.
Bigger shared multiples ($24, 36, \ldots$) exist, but $12$ is least.

steps.title: Finding the LCM
steps.intro: List, then take the first shared multiple.

steps.title: Find the LCM
steps.intro: Smallest number in both lists.

problems.explanation: $6$ is the first multiple of both.

steps.title: When LCM is just the product
beats: If two numbers share no factor but $1$ ($\text{GCF}=1$)...
...their LCM is simply the **product**.
$3$ and $5$ share only $1$, so $\text{LCM} = 3\times5 = 15$.
But $4$ and $6$ share a factor, so their LCM ($12$) is **less** than $24$.

steps.title: Product or less?
steps.intro: Check for a shared factor first.

steps.title: Least, not just any
steps.intro: Find the first shared multiple.

problems.prompt: Which is the LCM of $4$ and $6$?
problems.explanation: $24$ and $36$ are shared too, but $12$ is least.

steps.title: Adding fractions needs it
steps.body: To add $\tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{1}{6}$ you rewrite both over the **least common multiple** of $4$ and $6$, which is $12$: $\tfrac{3}{12} + \tfrac{2}{12} = \tfrac{5}{12}$. The LCM is exactly the least common denominator.

steps.title: Smallest shared multiple
steps.prompt: What is the LCM of $4$ and $6$?
steps.explanation: $12$ is the first multiple of both $4$ and $6$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **common multiple** appears in both numbers' lists.
If the numbers share no factor but $1$, the LCM is their **product**.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review the LCM with quick finds, the product shortcut, and a real-world timing problem.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: $48$ is a common multiple of $6$ and $8$. Is it the LCM?
problems.explanation: $24$ is also a common multiple and is smaller.

problems.prompt: Buses leave every $10$ min and every $15$ min, together now. When together again?

steps.title: You've finished "Least Common Multiple"
steps.body: Well done — you can list multiples, find where they first meet, and use the product shortcut when numbers share no factors. Last in this unit: Prime Factorization, where factor trees break numbers into their prime building blocks.

lessons.title: Prime Factorization
lessons.concreteComparison: Every whole number is built from prime “atoms.” Just as a LEGO model breaks down into individual bricks, $12$ breaks into $2\times2\times3$ — its **prime factorization**, the unique set of prime building blocks that multiply back to $12$.
lessons.objective: Write a composite number as a product of primes using a factor tree, and use exponents for repeated primes.
concept: Every composite number is a **product of primes** in exactly one way (apart from order).
Build a **factor tree**: split off a prime, then keep factoring what's left.
lessons.keyIdea: Prime factorization = a number written as a product of primes. Use a factor tree; write repeats with exponents.

commonMistakes.text: Stopping before every branch is prime.
commonMistakes.correction: Keep splitting until each end is prime: $4$ must become $2\times2$, not stay as $4$.
$2\times2\times2 = 2^3$; both equal $8$ — the exponent only counts how many times the prime appears.

steps.title: Break it into primes
steps.teach: Tap **Split once** to peel off the smallest prime factor, then keep going on what's left. When every end is prime (the round chips), you've found the prime factorization.

steps.title: Build a factor tree
beats: Split the number into any two factors.
Keep splitting any factor that isn't prime.
$6 = 2\times3$, so $12 = 2\times2\times3$.

steps.title: Write repeats as powers
beats: Count how many times each prime appears.
$12 = 2\times2\times3$ has two $2$s.
Write that as $2^2$.
So $12 = 2^2\times3$ — same value, shorter.

steps.title: Factoring into primes
steps.intro: Split until every end is prime.

steps.title: Find the factorization
steps.intro: Pick the all-primes product.

problems.explanation: $4$ and $10$ aren't prime; $2^2\times5$ is all primes.

steps.title: One number, one factorization
beats: No matter how you split, you reach the **same** primes.
$36 = 4\times9$ or $6\times6$ — both give $2^2\times3^2$.
Every number has exactly **one** prime factorization.
That's the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.

steps.title: Same answer, any path
steps.intro: Different first split, same primes.

steps.title: Spot the prime factorization
steps.intro: All factors must be prime.

problems.prompt: Which is the prime factorization of $45$?
$2\times2\times7$ is the prime factorization of which number?

steps.title: The lock on the internet
steps.body: Multiplying two big primes is easy, but pulling the product back apart into those primes is so slow that no computer can do it quickly. That one-way difficulty is what hides your messages and card numbers behind RSA encryption every day.

steps.title: Pick the primes
steps.prompt: What is the prime factorization of $12$?
steps.explanation: $2\times6$ and $3\times4$ still contain composites; $2^2\times3$ is all primes.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Every composite number is a unique **product of primes**.
Build a **factor tree**, splitting until every end is prime.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review prime factorization with factor trees, exponents, and a spot-the-real-factorization check.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Which is the prime factorization of $36$?
problems.explanation: The others still hold composite numbers.

problems.prompt: $2\times2\times7$ is the prime factorization of which number?
Which product is NOT a finished prime factorization?

steps.title: You've finished "Prime Factorization"
steps.body: Excellent — you can build a factor tree, write the primes with exponents, and you've seen why the answer is always unique. That wraps up Factors & Multiples!

practice.statement: List the first five multiples of $9$.

practice.solution: **Neither** — $1$ has only one factor (itself), so it fits no category.

testYourself.solution: $1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36$ — the numbers that divide $36$ with no remainder.

testYourself.statement: List the first four multiples of $8$.

testYourself.solution: **Prime** — nothing from $2$ up to $\sqrt{29}$ divides it.
$24$ — the smallest number both divide into.
